chapter.title: 创建太空探索技术公司

reader_page: docs/reports/readerlab-elon-chapter-loop-v0/reader/08_创建太空探索技术公司_阅读页.md
baseline_summary_trap: Do not reduce this chapter to an inspirational founder biography about optimism, starting SpaceX, failing three launches, succeeding on the fourth, winning a NASA contract, and finally building reusable rockets. The core issue is how a civilizational goal that the market will not naturally complete becomes an executable transportation system, cost metric, learning cycle, risk boundary, and commercial survival path.
previous_hard_issue: The previous chapter passed with no P0/P1/P2. Carry forward the discipline: do not pile up nonessential numeric detail; do not romanticize crisis, explosions, all-in risk, or extreme pressure; keep local chapter experience from becoming a whole-book conclusion.
allowed_lenses: civilizational goal translated into a transportation system
optimism as preserving an action entrance for a low-probability but important problem
greenhouse mission exposing the real bottleneck as launch cost
first-principles cost decomposition across suppliers, risk aversion, outsourcing, cost-plus incentives, and disposability
commercial survival path as oxygen for a distant mission
fast iteration with a hard safety boundary between uncrewed Starship tests and crewed Dragon operations
rapid, complete, reliable reusability as the real meaning of reusable rockets
cost per ton of payload as the compressed metric for a Mars city
source_anchors: The chapter distinguishes mass-market electric cars as likely to happen eventually from space civilization as not inevitable.
SpaceX begins from the question of why humans did not continue from Apollo to Mars, return to the Moon, or preserve independent human orbital capability.
The initial diagnosis was lack of exploration will, but the later diagnosis is that people thought the road was blocked; support arrives when a path appears possible.
The Mars greenhouse idea was meant to inspire public enthusiasm and NASA budget support through a vivid image of life on Mars.
After spacecraft, communications, and greenhouse costs were compressed, launch cost remained the blocker; failed Russian missile trips exposed the need to improve space transportation technology.
SpaceX was founded on a low-probability but nonzero chance; if startups did not push rocket technology, there would be no path.
Musk does not recommend space as a first startup because it is capital-intensive, high-risk, and advanced entrepreneurship.
Self-funding and delayed outside investment were tied to the absence of prior successes and the view that founders should risk their own money first.
The early business path used the known market for small and medium satellite launches before moving into cargo, Dragon, crew transport, and Starship.
Rocket cost is decomposed into legacy parts, risk aversion, multi-layer outsourcing, cost-plus incentives, and disposability.
The first three launches failed; the fourth launch and later NASA contract meant survival, not a clean victory.
Fast iteration has boundaries: early uncrewed Starship tests can learn from hardware loss, while crewed Dragon operations require extreme conservatism.
Starship's optimization chain runs from the shortest time to a Mars city, to the shortest time for a fully usable rocket, to the shortest time to orbital flight.
True reusability means rapid, complete, reliable reuse, not merely recovering hardware.
Cost per ton of payload is the compressed metric that decides whether a self-sustaining Mars city can move from aspiration to system.

P2: Optional: final sentence was slightly poetic; controller made it more engineering-oriented before landing.
Optional: early commercial-path section could be compressed, but this is not a landing blocker.

controller_decision: Land. Hard gates all passed, reader score is 12/12, and there are no P0/P1 issues. Optional P2 wording feedback was addressed lightly before landing. This remains chapter-level progress only and does not authorize full-book summary, final boss baseline comparison, or methodology drafting.
